statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
This is the easy version of the problem. The difference between the versions is that in this version, $ n \le 10^{5}, m \le 10^{4} $ . You can hack only if you solved all versions of this problem.

There are two strings $ s $ and $ t $ , both consisting of lowercase Latin letters. You also have an empty string $ p $ .

You can perform the following operation, which consists of several stages:

- choose any two integers $ l $ and $ r $ ( $ 1 \le l \le r \le |s| $ );
- copy the substring $ s_{l},\ldots, s_{r} $ and append it to the end of string $ p $ ;
- among the last $ r - l + 1 $ characters of string $ p $ , change at most one character to any lowercase Latin letter.

For example, if the string $ s = $ "dhhtyhwbsl" and $ p = $ "", you can choose $ l = 3, r = 6 $ and add "htyh" to the end of $ p $ , and then change the character "y" to "a", resulting in $ p = $ "htah".

Your task is to determine the minimum number of operations required for string $ p $ to become equal to $ t $ .

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains two integers $ n $ and $ m $ ( $ 1 \le n \le 10^{5} $ , $ 1 \le m \le 10^{4} $ ) â the lengths of strings $ s $ and $ t $ .

The second line of each test case contains the string $ s $ of length $ n $ , consisting of lowercase Latin letters.

The third line of each test case contains the string $ t $ of length $ m $ , consisting of lowercase Latin letters.

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 10^{5} $ , and the sum of $ m $ across all test cases does not exceed $ 10^{4} $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer â the answer to the problem.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
4
1 1
a
b
5 5
aaaaa
abzba
10 13
dhhtyhwbsl
htahbsehtyhzb
7 2
contest
on
Output
1
3
3
1

Note
In the first test case, you can take the substring $ ÂŦ\mathtt{a}Âŧ $ from the string $ s $ , change a single letter in this string to $ ÂŦ\mathtt{b}Âŧ $ , and append it to the end of the string $ p $ , resulting in $ p $ becoming equal to $ t $ .

In the second test case, $ t $ can be obtained in $ 3 $ operations as follows:

$$$
ÂŦ\mathtt{a\color{red}{b}}Âŧ + ÂŦ\mathtt{\color{red}{z}}Âŧ + ÂŦ\mathtt{\color{red}{b}a}Âŧ
$$$

The modified characters are highlighted in red.

In the third test case, the string $ t $ can be obtained as follows:

$$$
ÂŦ\mathtt{ht\color{red}{a}h}Âŧ + ÂŦ\mathtt{bs\color{red}{e}}Âŧ + ÂŦ\mathtt{htyh\color{red}{z}b}Âŧ
$$$

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2196E1 - Fuzzy Concatenation (Easy Version)
The following greedy algorithm works: we will find the longest prefix of the string $$$t$$$ such that this prefix is contained in $$$s$$$ as a substring (possibly with one character replaced), and then remove this prefix from $$$t$$$ and repeat until $$$t$$$ is empty. The number of such "pieces" will be the answer. This greediness is correct because if we take a prefix of shorter length, the next piece can only decrease in size, not increase.
Now we need to learn how to quickly solve the following problem:
For a fixed number $$$l$$$, find the maximum $$$r$$$ such that there exists a number $$$i$$$ and a string $$$c$$$, for which the string $$$t_l t_{l + 1} \ldots t_{i - 1} c t_{i + 1} \ldots t_{r}$$$ is a substring of the string $$$s$$$.
Solving this problem involves using root optimization. Let $$$B$$$ be the constant we chose for our root. Let $$$d$$$ denote $$$r - l + 1$$$ for optimal $$$i, c$$$ (in other words, how many characters we processed in one iteration of the greedy algorithm). We want to handle the case $$$d  \lt  B$$$ in $$$O(d)$$$ time.
Let's store the hash of each substring of the string $$$s$$$ of length at most $$$B$$$, where we have removed one of the elements. There are a total of $$$O(n \cdot B^2)$$$ such segments. Thus, for fixed $$$i, d$$$, where $$$d  \lt  B$$$, we can check in $$$O(1)$$$ time whether a given string is a substring of $$$s$$$ by verifying that the hash of the string without the character $$$c$$$ is in the set of precomputed hashes. This allows us to solve the case when $$$d  \lt  B$$$ in $$$O(d^2)$$$ time. We can iterate $$$i$$$ from $$$l$$$ to the maximum accumulated length and increase the optimal $$$d$$$ by exactly $$$1$$$ if we find this substring for the given $$$i$$$. Smaller $$$r$$$ values are not of interest to us. Overall, this part of the solution works in $$$O(m \cdot B)$$$ time, as we increase $$$r$$$ no more than $$$m$$$ times in total, and each check works in $$$d$$$ time, since there are at most $$$B$$$ interesting values of $$$i$$$ in each iteration.
If $$$d \geq B$$$, we can explicitly iterate over the starting point of the substring in the string $$$s$$$. Let's denote this starting point of the substring as $$$j$$$. Let $$$d_1$$$ be $$$\operatorname{lcp}(s_j \ldots s_n, t_l \ldots t_m)$$$. Then for a fixed starting point $$$j$$$, we can add at most $$$d_1 + 1 + \operatorname{lcp}(s_{j + d_1 + 1} \ldots s_n, t_{l + d_1 + 1} \ldots t_m)$$$. The $$$\operatorname{lcp}$$$ can be easily computed in $$$O(1)$$$ time using a suffix array for the string $$$s\text{\#}t$$$. Thus, this part of the solution works in at most $$$O(\frac{n \cdot m}{B})$$$ time, as this search algorithm will be run no more than $$$\frac{m}{B}$$$ times, and the iteration itself works in $$$O(n)$$$ time.
The asymptotic complexity of the solution is $$$O(n \cdot B^2 + \frac{n \cdot m}{B} + m \cdot B)$$$. With $$$B=50$$$, this solution should fit well within the problem's constraints.
There is also a solution that runs in $$$26 \cdot m \cdot \log^2(n + m) + \log(n) \cdot (n + m)$$$, which is based on a complete solution but uses hashes
